pmos

Fundraising & VCs — Primer

Created using pmos‑learnkit

Primer · Cross-Functional Skills. Most product people meet fundraising sideways — a term sheet lands, a board seat appears, the roadmap suddenly answers to someone new. This primer explains the machine behind that: how startups raise money and how the investors who fund them actually think. It is written for product managers in plain English. It covers what venture capital is; how venture funds make money; the power law that drives every investor decision; the funding stages from pre-seed to growth; pre-money and post-money valuation; dilution and cap tables; SAFEs and convertible notes; the priced round; term sheets; liquidation preferences; what investors look for; what a PM should take from all of it; and where to go next. Every term of art is defined on first use, and every acronym is spelled out. Source provenance: curation-consensus across the startup-finance canon — the Wikipedia finance corpus, Wall Street Prep, Carta-adjacent practitioner guides (CRV, EQT, Forum Ventures, Hustle Fund), and Silicon Valley Bank. 17 verified sources.

What venture capital is

Venture capital (VC) is money invested in young, fast-growing companies in exchange for a slice of ownership. Wikipedia defines it as "a form of private equity financing provided by firms or funds to startup, early-stage, and emerging companies," usually ones "based on an innovative technology or business model." The investor does not lend the money and expect repayment with interest. They buy shares and bet those shares become worth far more later.

It is a high-risk trade by design. As Wikipedia puts it, "because startups face high uncertainty, VC investments have high rates of failure." Investors accept that many of their bets will go to zero, because the goal is not a steady return on each one — it is a rare enormous return on a few. That logic shapes everything else in this primer.

Not every early backer is a fund. An angel investor is, per Wikipedia, "an individual who provides capital to a business or businesses, including startups, usually in exchange for convertible debt or ownership equity." Angels differ from venture capitalists in a key way: they "typically invest their funds (unlike venture capitalists, who manage the pooled money of others in a professionally managed fund)" and they back companies "at a very early stage." Beyond cash, angels often bring "feedback, advice, and contacts."

How venture funds make money

To understand investor behavior, follow the money one level up. A venture fund is not the firm's own cash — it is a pool raised from outside investors. Those outside investors are the limited partners (LPs): pension funds, university endowments, wealthy families. The people who run the fund and pick the investments are the general partners (GPs). Qubit Capital's example makes the split concrete: "The GP commits $1M of their own capital, and LPs contribute $99M."

GPs get paid two ways, known as "2 and 20." Qubit Capital describes the "two" as "the annual management fee, set at 2% of committed capital, paid regardless of fund performance to cover operations" — money that covers "salaries, rent, and deal sourcing." The "twenty" is the real prize: "carried interest: the GP's 20% share of profits once returns clear the hurdle rate."

Carried interest, or "carry," is the investment manager's cut of the gains. Wikipedia defines it as "a share of the profits of an investment paid to the investment manager," historically "20% in private equity buyout and venture investments." Crucially, GPs only earn carry after the LPs get their money back plus a minimum return called the hurdle rate or preferred return: Wikipedia notes "the customary hurdle rate is 7% to 9% per annum." A fund that merely breaks even pays its partners almost nothing. That is why investors push for huge outcomes, not safe ones.

The power law that drives every decision

The single most important idea in venture is the power law: returns are wildly lopsided. Hustle Fund states it plainly — "Power law in startup investing means that a teeny tiny percentage of companies will deliver massive returns, and most others will return little or nothing." A handful of winners pay for everything else.

This is not a flaw investors tolerate; it is the engine. Hustle Fund calls it "the fundamental economic model that makes venture capital work," because "if just one of your companies is a huge success (and I mean huge), that will more than make up for all those other losses." The example is striking: a $5,000 seed-round stake in Uber, held to its public listing, would have become $25 million — a 5,000-times return.

The power law explains investor behavior that otherwise looks irrational. A VC would rather fund a risky company that could be worth a hundred times its money than a safe one that might double. A "fine" outcome does not move a fund; only an outlier does. So when an investor asks how big your market could be, or pushes you to grow faster than feels comfortable, they are not being greedy — they are doing the only math that works for them.

The funding stages

Startups raise money in a sequence of rounds, each tied to how far the company has come. The earliest is seed money. Wikipedia defines it as "the earliest financial support a new project receives, often arriving at a point when an idea exists but nothing concrete has been built yet," coming from "friends and family funding, seed venture capital funds, angel funding, and crowdfunding" and paying for "preliminary operations such as market research and product development."

After seed come the lettered rounds, and each has a job. Silicon Valley Bank ties capital to milestones: Series A money is to "fine-tune your product or service" and "expand your workforce"; Series B is to "grow your operations," "meet customer demands," and "expand to new markets"; and Series C and beyond lets a company "build new products, reach new markets and even acquire other startups."

The numbers climb fast. Visible.vc reports that a pre-seed round "is generally the first round of institutional capital that a startup raises," typically $100,000 to $5M; Series A "averaged $18.7 million as of 2024," with investors who "typically purchase 10-30% of the company"; Series B "averages $30M or more"; and Series C runs $30–$100M with valuations "typically exceeding $100M." Each round is bigger, at a higher price, for a more proven company.

Pre-money and post-money valuation

Every priced round hangs on one number: what the company is worth. Investors and founders state it two ways, and confusing them is a costly rookie mistake. Wikipedia defines pre-money valuation as "the valuation of a company or asset prior to an investment or financing." It is the agreed worth before any new cash arrives.

The post-money valuation is the worth right after the money lands. Wikipedia notes that "the company will have a valuation after the investment that is equal to the pre-money valuation plus the cash amount." The formula is simply Pre-money valuation = Post-money valuation − Investment amount.

The reason this matters is that ownership is calculated on the post-money number. If a company is valued at $8M pre-money and raises $2M, the post-money valuation is $10M, and the new investor owns $2M ÷ $10M, or 20 percent. Negotiating "valuation" without saying which one you mean can quietly hand over far more of the company than intended — the same dollars buy a different slice depending on the base.

Dilution and the cap table

Every time a company sells new shares, existing owners get a smaller slice. That is dilution. CRV defines it as "the reduction in your ownership percentage that occurs when your company issues new shares, even though your absolute share count stays the same." You still hold the same number of shares; they just represent less of a bigger whole.

CRV's worked example shows the mechanics. Founders holding 8,000,000 of 10,000,000 shares own 80 percent. The company issues 2,500,000 new shares for a Series A, so the founders now own 8,000,000 of 12,500,000 — 8,000,000 out of 12,500,000 (64%). Their count never changed, but their percentage fell sixteen points. One subtle trap: the option pool, the shares reserved for future employees. CRV warns that when a pool is created before a round closes, "founders bear the cost while incoming investors remain unaffected."

All of this ownership is tracked in the cap table, short for capitalization table. EQT defines cap tables as "an essential tool for understanding and managing company equity, financial planning, and fundraising" that "record a company's ownership structure, detailing who owns what stake and its value." Founders keep it current to "manage potential dilution" across future rounds.

SAFEs and convertible notes

Pricing a company is hard when it is brand new, so the earliest money often skips valuation for now and uses an instrument that converts to shares later. The most common is the SAFE, short for Simple Agreement for Future Equity. Wall Street Prep defines it as "a form of early-stage startup financing introduced by Y Combinator in 2013," with "no interest or maturity date."

A SAFE carries two levers that reward early risk. The valuation cap, says Cake Equity, "sets the maximum valuation at which the investor's investment will convert into equity," pricing the conversion at whichever is lower — the cap or the next round's actual valuation. The discount lets the early money "convert into equity at a discounted price compared to the price paid by new investors." Wall Street Prep gives the intuition: "a 20% discount means the SAFE investor gets shares at 80% of the new round's price." When the priced round finally happens, "the SAFE note holders will then become shareholders."

The older cousin is the convertible note. The Startup Law Blog defines it as "a short-term debt instrument that converts into equity — typically preferred stock — when a startup raises a priced funding round." The defining difference: "a convertible note is debt — it accrues interest and has a maturity date — while a SAFE is not debt, has no interest or maturity, and only converts on a triggering event." That maturity date is real pressure — a note that has not converted by its deadline can force an awkward conversation.

The priced round

Eventually a startup raises a priced round — a real valuation is set, and the company sells newly created shares at a fixed price per share. This is when SAFEs and notes convert into actual equity, and when the pre-money-versus-post-money math from earlier does its work to set everyone's ownership.

A priced round is the most visible source of dilution because it mints new shares for the incoming investors. CRV describes exactly this: closing a round means the company "issues 2,500,000 new Series A shares" so that existing holders' percentages mechanically drop, even as the company is now worth more. The shares sold are usually preferred stock — a class that sits ahead of the founders' and employees' common stock when money is paid out, which is the bridge to the next two topics.

For a product manager, the priced round is the moment the company's ambitions get repriced and re-owned. New investors at this stage have bought a real stake at a real price, and Wikipedia's reminder holds: the post-money valuation "is equal to the pre-money valuation plus the cash amount," and every ownership percentage flows from that single agreed number.

Term sheets

The deal starts with a term sheet. Wikipedia defines it as "a bullet-point document outlining the material terms and conditions of a potential business agreement, establishing the basis for future negotiations." It is short, but it sets the shape of everything that follows.

One common surprise: a term sheet is mostly a promise to keep talking, not a contract. Wikipedia notes term sheets "may be either binding or non-binding," and once signed the document "guides legal counsel in the preparation of a proposed 'definitive agreement'." The lengthy legal paperwork comes later; the term sheet is the blueprint.

Term sheets split into two kinds of terms, and it helps to read them with that lens. Economic terms decide who gets how much money — valuation, the option pool, and the liquidation preference covered next. Control terms decide who gets to make decisions — board seats, voting rights, and vetoes over big moves. Founders often fixate on valuation, but the control terms are what change daily life inside the company, including how much room a product team actually has.

Liquidation preferences

The term that most often catches founders off guard is the liquidation preference. Wikipedia calls it "one of the primary economic terms of a venture finance investment" that "establishes that certain investors receive their investment money back first before other company owners" when the company is sold or wound down. Investors get paid before founders and employees.

The preference comes in two flavors that pay out very differently. With a participating preference, Wikipedia says investors "receive their preference first and are then entitled to a share of any remaining funds based on their ownership" — they get their money back and then share the rest, sometimes called "double dipping." With a non-participating preference, investors take "either their preference amount only... or else their proportionate share of distributions... whichever is greater" — they pick the better of the two, but not both.

This is why a high valuation can hide a worse deal. A founder who accepts a big headline number alongside an aggressive participating preference can walk away with less in a modest sale than they would have with a lower valuation and a clean 1x non-participating term. The structure of the payout can matter more than the size of the valuation.

What investors look for

Given the power law and the way funds get paid, investors are not hunting for safe, steady companies. They are hunting for the rare one that could return the whole fund. Forum Ventures distills early-stage evaluation into four criteria: "Team – The founder's lived experience, grit, and resilience"; "Product – Proof that your solution solves a real problem"; "Market – A scalable market opportunity with room for growth"; and "Traction – Signals of momentum, whether it's revenue, customer feedback, or product adoption."

Early on, one of those dominates. Forum Ventures is blunt: "at the earliest stages, the founding team is the single most important factor in an investment decision." There is little product or revenue to judge, so investors bet on the people. As a company matures, the weight shifts toward traction and market — the evidence that the bet is paying off.

The market criterion connects straight back to the power law. An investor needs an outcome big enough to matter to a fund, and an outcome can only be as big as the market allows. That is why "how big can this get?" is the question behind so many investor conversations — a great team in a small market cannot produce a fund-returning result.

What product managers should take from this

You do not need to negotiate a term sheet to be affected by one. The point of this primer is to read the forces that shape your roadmap. Three habits help.

First, connect product strategy to the investor's math. When leadership pushes for faster growth or a bigger market wedge, that pressure usually traces to the power law and the fund's need for an outsized outcome. The product bets that open the largest markets are the ones that align with how the company is financed.

Second, understand that funding stage sets your constraints. A seed-stage company is searching for product-market fit, so the roadmap should serve learning and proof. A Series B company has, in Silicon Valley Bank's words, money to "grow your operations" and "expand to new markets" — a different mandate entirely. Knowing the stage tells you what kind of work the company can afford to value.

Third, read the control terms, not just the headlines. A new investor often brings a board seat and approval rights, which means roadmap and budget decisions may now answer to someone new. Recognizing who holds those rights — a direct consequence of the term-sheet structure above — explains a great deal about how decisions actually get made after a round closes.

Where this connects — adjacent topics

These pointers are starting points, not lessons — each is a topic worth its own primer.

  • Financial metrics and unit economics — the CAC, LTV, and growth numbers investors scrutinize as "traction" before they write a check.
  • Corporate finance and valuation — the discounting and cash-flow methods behind the valuations a term sheet sets.
  • Equity compensation — how option pools, vesting, and strike prices turn the cap table into employee ownership.
  • Board management and governance — what the control terms in a term sheet mean for how a company is actually run.
  • Go-to-market strategy — how the market-size story investors fund translates into a plan to capture it.
pmos Source: docs/pmos/primer/2026-06-14_fundraising-and-vcs.html · Created using pmos‑learnkit
